data.title: Agios outlines path to profitability and targets $45M-$50M in 2026 PK deficiency revenue as AQVESME U.S. launch progresses
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Agios Pharmaceuticals (AGIO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Goff emphasized the company's 2026 strategic priorities, stating, "we're focused on executing a high-impact launch of AQVESME for the treatment of thalassemia in the U.S." He highlighted plans to expand the PK activation franchise into new indications, including sickle cell disease and lower-risk myelodysplastic syndrome, with key catalysts expected in 2026.
  • Goff noted, "PYRUKYND delivered $20 million in net revenue in the fourth quarter, bringing full year 2025 revenue to $54 million, reflecting robust year-on-year growth." He reported FDA approval for AQVESME just before year-end and stated, "the U.S. thalassemia launch is underway."
  • Goff pointed to a strong balance sheet: "We continue to operate from a position of strength, ending the year with approximately $1.2 billion in cash, providing flexibility to maximize the AQVESME thalassemia U.S. launch, pursue the path forward for mitapivat in sickle cell disease and continue to advance our pipeline programs."
  • CFO Cecilia Jones stated, "Fourth quarter worldwide PYRUKYND revenue was $20 million, an increase of 86% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024 and a sequential increase of 55% compared to $13 million in the third quarter of 2025."
  • Jones added, "In 2026, we expect U.S. PK deficiency revenues to be in the range of $45 million to $50 million."
  • Chief Commercial Officer Tsveta Milanova described early AQVESME launch progress: "As of January 30, we have seen 44 prescriptions written by REMS-certified physicians in the U.S., which reflects strong early recognition of AQVESME clinical value and excellent execution by our field teams."

Outlook

  • U.S. PK deficiency revenues are projected in the range of $45 million to $50 million for 2026, according to CFO Jones.
  • Operating expenses in 2026 are anticipated to be "roughly flat with 2025," reflecting investments in the AQVESME U.S. launch and gated investment for sickle cell disease.
  • Management reiterated their commitment to "a clear path to profitability through our existing commercial presence in thalassemia and PK deficiency."

Financial Results

  • PYRUKYND net revenue reached $20 million in Q4 2025, up from $13 million in Q3 2025.
  • U.S. revenue in Q4 was $16 million, driven by commercial focus in PK deficiency, an additional ordering week, and favorable gross to net adjustment.
  • Ex-U.S. revenue was $4 million, attributed to inventory stocking in Europe, with a sequential decline expected into Q1 2026.
  • Cost of sales for Q4 was $1.9 million. R&D expenses were $88.1 million, up $5.3 million year-over-year. SG&A was $51.6 million and flat year-on-year.
  • Cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities at year-end totaled approximately $1.2 billion.

Q&A

  • Gregory Renza, Truist Securities: Asked about AQVESME launch translation from prescriptions to revenue. Tsveta Milanova responded, "it takes about 10 to 12 weeks for us to convert prescriptions to treatment initiations and ultimately revenues. So we anticipate in Q1, the majority of the prescriptions to turn into treatment initiation."
  • Samantha Semenkow, Citi: Asked about AQVESME script cadence and outcomes from the planned sNDA meeting for sickle cell disease. Milanova said, "we are very encouraged with what we see in terms of demand in the early stages of the launch," and Gheuens stated, "the goal for that meeting is, of course, to gain insights in the regulatory pathways that we can use for mitapivat."
  • Alec Stranahan, BofA Securities: Inquired about bottlenecks in the 10-12 week conversion timeline and Tebapivat dosing in MDS. Milanova cited insurance authorization and liver testing as main drivers, while Gheuens explained the rationale for higher dosing in MDS.
  • Marc Frahm, TD Cowen: Asked about REMS certification progress and potential for accelerated approval in sickle cell. Milanova reported, "Physicians don't see the REMS as barrier to prescribing." Gheuens noted, "we are very, very excited about the data as it stands."
  • Other analysts probed physician profiles, payer dynamics, potential for sickle cell approval, and SG&A scaling.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive but probing tone, with focus on AQVESME launch metrics, revenue recognition, and regulatory milestones. Questions were direct and sought clarity on conversion rates, market access, and approval timelines.
  • Management conveyed confidence, especially in prepared remarks, using phrases such as "we are really encouraged" and "I feel very confident the team in any scenario will be very well prepared." In Q&A, management remained transparent and detailed, but cautious around specific guidance and approval timing.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from preparatory and anticipatory to execution-focused, buoyed by early AQVESME launch progress and increased revenue guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance evolved from expectations of robust revenue growth in 2025 (previous quarter) to more specific projections for 2026, including $45 million to $50 million in U.S. PK deficiency revenue.
  • Strategic focus shifted from launch readiness for thalassemia to execution of the AQVESME launch and expansion into sickle cell disease and MDS.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from regulatory process and REMS program speculation to launch metrics, payer dynamics, and operational execution.
  • Management’s confidence increased, supported by explicit early launch data and a strong financial position.
  • Key metrics such as PYRUKYND revenue rose markedly quarter-over-quarter, and cash reserves remained robust.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted anticipated sequential decline in ex-U.S. revenue in Q1 2026 due to inventory stocking dynamics in Europe.
  • Regulatory risks remain, with several approvals pending in global markets and the outcome of an upcoming pre-sNDA meeting for sickle cell disease.
  • The time lag from prescription to treatment initiation (10-12 weeks) and dependency on insurance authorization and liver testing may impact near-term revenue recognition.
  • Analysts flagged the importance of payer dynamics, SG&A scalability for future launches, and the ability to convert early demand into sustained growth.

Final Takeaway

Agios highlighted a pivotal transition into 2026, underscored by the high-impact U.S. launch of AQVESME in thalassemia, strong early commercial execution, and robust financial discipline. Management outlined a clear trajectory toward profitability, emphasizing a projected $45 million to $50 million in 2026 U.S. PK deficiency revenue, and continued advancement in its rare disease pipeline. Early physician and patient engagement, coupled with strategic capital allocation, support the company’s foundation for long-term value creation and expansion into new indications.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cognex targets 25% adjusted EBITDA margin run rate by end of 2026 amid cost reductions and portfolio optimization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cognex Corporation (CGNX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Matt Moschner, President, CEO & Director, opened by noting "2025 marked a return to profitable growth for Cognex, with constant currency revenue growth of 8% year-over-year and adjusted EPS growth of 38%." He highlighted momentum in logistics and factory automation, and emphasized progress on three strategic objectives: leading in AI for industrial machine vision, delivering the best customer experience, and aiming to double the customer base within five years.
  • Moschner described several AI-driven product launches, including DataMan 290, In-Sight 8900, OneVision, and SLX, stating these "strengthen our position within approximately $3.2 billion of our $7 billion served market" and support market share gains.
  • Moschner announced the exit from product lines generating $22 million of no growth or low margin revenue and a planned $35 million to $40 million in annualized cost reductions by year-end 2026, saying, "We remain focused on increasing productivity in key areas...using new digital tools, software development, using AI-assisted code generation, and automating back-office processes."
  • Dennis Fehr, Senior VP of Finance & CFO, stated, "We ended 2025 with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 20.7%, excluding the one-time benefit from the Commercial Partnership. We achieved our first milestone, reaching greater than 20% a full year ahead of plan, driven by focused execution and strong cost discipline."
  • Fehr added, "We are raising our through-cycle adjusted EBITDA margin range to 25%, 31% from the prior 20%, 30%. Our through-cycle revenue CAGR remains 13%, 14%, and we continue to expect greater than 100% free cash flow conversion."

Outlook

  • Fehr guided Q1 2026 revenue between $235 million and $255 million, "representing growth of approximately 13% at the midpoint against a weak comp." Adjusted EBITDA margin is expected between 19% and 22%, with the midpoint "representing an increase of 370 basis points year-over-year."
  • Adjusted EPS for Q1 is expected in the range of $0.22 to $0.26, with the midpoint "representing approximately 50% year-over-year growth."
  • The company targets a 25% adjusted EBITDA margin run rate exiting 2026 and "at least 20% adjusted EPS growth."

Financial Results

  • For Q4 2025, Fehr reported an adjusted EBITDA margin of 22.7%, "representing an increase of 420 basis points year-over-year, the sixth consecutive quarter of year-over-year expansion."
  • Adjusted EPS increased 35% year-over-year to $0.27. GAAP diluted EPS was $0.19, up 18% from the prior year.
  • Revenue increased 10% year-over-year and 9% on a constant currency basis. Americas revenue expanded 11%, Europe 13%, and Greater China 7%.
  • For the full year 2025, revenue was $994 million, adjusted EBITDA margin 21.5%, and adjusted EPS $1.02. Free cash flow reached $237 million, up 77% year-over-year.
  • The company returned $206 million to shareholders, with $151 million in share repurchases. Net cash and investments totaled $642 million at year-end.

Q&A

  • Michael Anastasiou, TD Cowen, asked about the timing and impact of the $22 million revenue divestments. Fehr responded the majority "is related to that Japan-focused trading business...currently expecting to close that transaction by the end of the year or within the second quarter," impacting packaging and logistics verticals.
  • Anastasiou also queried the core vs. non-core determination. Moschner explained, "where do we have core IP, core skill...what is the size of that market? What's the growth potential? What's the relative profit pool, profitability, and our ability to capture those profits?"
  • Joseph Ritchie, Goldman Sachs, probed the cost reduction program. Fehr replied, "the largest lever is OpEx efficiency...really the headwind which we see is on the mix side, and then we would see, perhaps, as we mentioned, as well, some favorability from pricing, but I would say that it's probably really a smaller piece."
  • Jamie Cook, Truist Securities, sought clarification on organic top-line assumptions for 2026 and the portfolio optimization process. Fehr stated, "mid-single to a high single-digit range is kind of what we would say from today's perspective," and Moschner said, "as it relates to the portfolio analysis, that's it."
  • Brooke Alexandra Goldman, BNP Paribas, asked about end markets. Moschner described consumer electronics momentum as "broad-based" and logistics as "a very exciting market for us...capping our eighth quarter of double-digit growth."
  • Kenneth Newman, KeyBanc, inquired about memory costs and cost benefits cadence. Moschner stated no material impact expected from memory prices and Fehr said the majority of cost benefits should show up by Q3 2026.
  • Thomas Moll, Stephens, questioned automotive trends. Moschner noted "relatively more activity and strength" in the U.S., with Europe remaining weak and Asia mixed.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on cost reductions, portfolio optimization, and end market growth, reflecting a slightly positive but focused tone on execution and follow-through.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, repeatedly referencing milestones achieved ahead of schedule and expressing conviction in achieving margin and growth targets. Moschner emphasized, "We remain focused on increasing productivity...and automating back-office processes."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive regarding successful execution and future targets, while analysts' sentiment shifted from cautious optimism to moderate confidence in the company's turnaround.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from "framework" and caution based on macro indicators in Q3 to a more definitive target of 25% adjusted EBITDA margin run rate by end of 2026.
  • The Q4 call included more explicit details on portfolio divestments and cost reductions, compared to a prior focus on sales transformation and product launches.
  • Analysts' questions in Q4 centered on concrete timing, impact of cost actions, and sustaining growth in key verticals, versus broader strategic questions in Q3.
  • Management's confidence increased, referencing milestones reached ahead of plan and expanded financial targets, in contrast to more measured comments in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted macroeconomic uncertainty and uneven demand as ongoing challenges.
  • The exit of low-margin product lines and ongoing cost reductions present execution risks, with Fehr stating, "we remain focused on increasing productivity in key areas...and automating back-office processes."
  • Analysts questioned the durability of growth trends and the cadence of cost reduction realization.

Final Takeaway

Cognex closed 2025 with accelerating revenue growth, significant margin expansion, and robust free cash flow generation. The company is executing a focused portfolio optimization, targeting $35 million to $40 million in annualized cost reductions and a 25% adjusted EBITDA margin run rate exiting 2026. With a strengthened product portfolio, streamlined go-to-market strategy, and increased confidence in customer acquisition, Cognex positions itself for continued profitable growth and shareholder value creation through 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Instacart sees double-digit growth across key metrics and nearly $1B in revenue
data.description:
Maplebear (CART), more commonly known as Instacart, is rallying in Thursday’s after-hours trading as the company’s fourth quarter results reflected double-digit growth across almost all metrics, a higher customer retention rate, and nearly $1B in revenue.

During the final quarter of the year, Instacart realized a 14% growth in gross transaction value ((GTV)), 16% increase in orders, a 13% gain in transaction revenue, and a 10% gain in ad revenue, all of which contributed to a 12% increase in total revenue to a better-than-expected $992M (versus $970M estimates). 

The company also noted that new customer retention rate was the strongest it’s been since early 2023, and 10M customers placed at least one order in December alone.

Profits, however, were curbed by a $60M settlement with the Federal Trade Commission in connection with claims that Instacart (CART) used deceptive practices pertaining to its membership fees and return policies. As a result, the company earned $0.30 per share, down from $0.53 a year earlier and $0.22 below expectations.

Looking ahead to Q1, Instacart (CART) sees GTV rising 11% to 13% to a range of $10.125B to $10.275B, and adjusted EBITDA up 15% to 19% to be between $280M and $290M.

“Our strategy is working. Our operating fundamentals are strong. And our teams are executing well across our marketplace, enterprise platform, and advertising and data offerings. We have multiple engines for growth and multiple levers to drive efficiency and reinvestment,” Instacart CEO Chris Rogers said in a letter to shareholders.

Shares are up more than 15% in after-hours trading.

data.title: Leggett & Platt outlines $3.8B–$4.0B 2026 sales target amid portfolio streamlining and restructuring benefits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Leggett & Platt (LEG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President, CEO & Executive Chairman Karl Glassman highlighted that "we delivered on those priorities and made significant progress to position the business to accelerate when residential end markets turn." The company has "substantially completed the restructuring plan we launched in early 2024," achieving greater EBIT benefit at lower cost than expected. Glassman noted the divestiture of the Aerospace business, with after-tax proceeds used to retire commercial paper and reduce leverage, moving closer to a long-term target of 2x.

  • Glassman indicated continued focus on "improving our cost structure and enhance profitability across our businesses." Highlights include growth in semi-finished products like Eco-Base and ComfortCore, expansion in Specialty Foam, progress in Automotive innovation, a new Home Furniture facility in Vietnam, and deeper penetration into specialty textile markets.

  • Glassman acknowledged ongoing weak residential markets but expressed confidence in capturing incremental volume when demand recovers. The restructuring and portfolio simplification are expected to yield sustainable improvements in profitability and cash flow.

  • Executive VP & CFO Benjamin Burns stated, "Fourth quarter sales were $939 million, down 11% versus the fourth quarter of 2024," citing sales weakness in certain customers, retailer changes, and soft residential demand. He reported, "Fourth quarter EBIT was $32 million and adjusted EBIT was $48 million," and "Fourth quarter earnings per share was $0.18. On an adjusted basis, fourth quarter EPS was $0.22, a 5% increase from fourth quarter 2024 adjusted EPS of $0.21."

  • Burns summarized that "operating cash flow was $338 million, an increase of $33 million versus 2024," with year-end net debt to adjusted EBITDA decreasing from 3.8x to 2.4x. He confirmed the restructuring plan delivered "a full run rate of approximately $70 million of EBIT benefit."

Outlook

  • Burns provided 2026 guidance: "2026 sales are expected to be $3.8 billion to $4.0 billion or down 1% to 6% versus 2025."

  • Volume is projected to be "flat to down low single digits," with Bedding Products and Specialized Products segments both expected to see "down low single digits" in volume, and Furniture, Flooring and Textile Products "flat."

  • 2026 earnings per share are expected to be in the range of "$0.92 to $1.38," with adjusted EPS forecast at "$1 to $1.20."

  • Full year adjusted EBIT margin is expected between "6.3% to 7.0%."

  • Cash from operations is guided to be "$225 million to $275 million in 2026," and CapEx is projected at "$100 million to $115 million."

Financial Results

  • Segment results for Q4: Bedding Products sales decreased 11%, Specialized Products declined 21% (mainly due to Aerospace divestiture), and Furniture, Flooring and Textile Products were down 3%.

  • Full year 2025 sales decreased 7% to $4.05 billion, with adjusted EBIT at $263 million and adjusted EPS at $1.05. Burns noted "adjusted working capital as a percentage of annualized sales of 11.6%, a decrease of 140 basis points versus 2024."

  • Debt was reduced by $376 million in 2025, supported by divestiture proceeds and real estate sales.

  • Restructuring benefits in 2025 were $63 million, with another $5 million expected in 2026, totaling a $70 million run rate.

Q&A

  • Susan Maklari, Goldman Sachs, asked about restructuring benefits and margin impacts. Glassman confirmed ongoing operational improvement opportunities and noted, "there are no divestitures of BUs being contemplated," while Burns stated, "we had really in 2025, $63 million of benefits that are flowing through, and we expect roughly another $5 million in 2026."

  • Maklari inquired about Bedding outlook and housing's impact. Glassman stated, "there is no expectation of macro market recovery in our 2026 forecast," and J. Hagale explained that guidance reflects "a continuation of what we really saw in the back half of the year."

  • Alessandra Jimenez, Raymond James, asked about U.S. Spring volumes and supply chain disruptions. J. Hagale attributed Q4 volume decline to a customer consolidation, and R. Smith described specific disruptions in Automotive, stating, "all those issues that we faced last quarter are really behind us now."

  • Maklari requested segment-specific guidance. Burns detailed: Bedding sales and volume down low single digits, margins up 150 basis points; Specialized Products sales and volume down low single digits, margins down 150 basis points; Furniture, Flooring and Textile Products sales and volumes flat, margins flat.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral tone, focusing on detailed operational execution and cost management, with cautious interest in segment outlooks and restructuring progress.

  • Management's prepared remarks were confident, particularly in restructuring achievements and balance sheet improvements. During Q&A, management was measured and transparent, emphasizing realistic guidance and the absence of market recovery assumptions. Glassman cautioned, "we are not doing that" regarding forecasting a strong recovery, and Hagale stated, "we don't feel good about building any type of recovery into guidance."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained pragmatic, shifting from cautious optimism about stabilization to a more grounded stance on continued demand weakness. Analyst tone was consistent, probing for clarity on cost actions and market trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter continues the theme of demand softness in residential markets, with management explicitly excluding market recovery from guidance, in contrast to previous quarters where some stabilization was noted.

  • Guidance for 2026 sales ($3.8B–$4.0B) reflects a slightly lower range than previous full-year guidance for 2025. Adjusted EPS guidance ($1–$1.20) remains tight, with continued focus on restructuring benefits and operational efficiency.

  • Analysts' focus shifted from stabilization signs to detailed questions about margin drivers, segment-specific trends, and capital allocation priorities.

  • Management confidence is now centered on cost control and operational discipline rather than external market recovery.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing demand uncertainty in residential markets and anticipated continued volume declines in certain segments.

  • Specific risks include soft consumer confidence, affordability headwinds, and persistent weak housing trends.

  • Glassman stated, "we have no ability to predict when" demand will recover, and Hagale noted that "the big drivers being housing, consumer confidence and general affordability are the big factors that we're watching."

  • Analysts raised concerns about lapping customer disruptions, supply chain risks, and the impact of macroeconomic conditions on sales and working capital.

Final Takeaway

Leggett & Platt’s fourth quarter 2025 call demonstrated the company’s successful execution of its restructuring plan and significant debt reduction, with management reiterating a disciplined approach to cost structure and operational efficiency amid ongoing demand weakness. Strategic portfolio simplification and targeted investments in product innovation and geographic expansion position the company to capitalize when markets recover. While 2026 guidance anticipates continued volume and sales headwinds, management remains focused on delivering sustainable profitability and shareholder value through balance sheet strength and operational excellence.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tripadvisor outlines shift to experiences-first model, expects marketplace revenue to reach two-thirds of total in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tripadvisor (TRIP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Matthew Goldberg stated the company achieved record high revenue of $1.9 billion in 2025, with "10% revenue growth in Experiences and 22% growth at TheFork, offsetting legacy revenue declines of 8% in our Hotels & Other segment." Goldberg emphasized, "Tripadvisor Group is fundamentally different today than it was 3 years ago," highlighting a strategic shift from SEO-dependent legacy offerings to a focus on the Experiences marketplace. He added, "In 2025, our marketplace businesses represented 61% of group revenue and 35% of adjusted EBITDA."

  • Goldberg announced, "We've decided to explore strategic alternatives for TheFork as part of our broader portfolio review," with the intent to potentially unlock additional shareholder value and increase capacity for capital return or Experiences investment.

  • The company reported a streamlined corporate structure and a clear priority to "extend our leadership position in Experiences globally, leverage our differentiated assets to position ourselves for an AI-enabled future and simplify our legacy offerings while we continue to evaluate strategic options."

  • CFO Mike Noonan stated, "Q4 consolidated revenue was $411 million, flat with a year ago and in line with our expectations. Revenue growth in Experiences and TheFork came in at the high end of our guidance range, but was offset by slightly lower revenue performance in Hotels & Other. Full year consolidated revenue was $1.9 billion or 3% growth."

Outlook

  • Noonan said, "For the full year, we expect modest consolidated revenue growth which reflects the ongoing mix shift we are driving towards our growth marketplace businesses." He projected that "marketplace businesses to continue to grow meaningfully and represent approximately 2/3 of our consolidated revenue as we exit 2026. Experiences revenue alone is expected to comprise over half of our consolidated revenue."

  • For 2026, the company expects Experiences segment "revenue growth in the low teens" and TheFork to achieve "revenue growth in the low to mid-teens." Hotels & Other is forecasted for "mid- to high-teens revenue declines."

  • Noonan detailed, "We expect to deliver flat to modest margin expansion alongside mid-single-digit growth EBITDA growth. Driven by our marketplace businesses and the in-year impact from our cost savings program," with marketplace businesses anticipated to contribute "approximately 50% of our overall EBITDA, up from 35% in 2025."

  • For Q1 2026, the company expects "consolidated revenue to be down by 3% to 5% year-over-year," but highlighted anticipated "consolidated revenue acceleration throughout the year as our marketplace business continued to increase their share of group revenue mix."

Financial Results

  • Goldberg reported, "Our gross booking value, or GBV, is rapidly approaching $5 billion," with Experiences adjusted EBITDA margins expanding to 10% in 2025.

  • Noonan shared, "Q4 Experiences revenue grew 10% to $204 million, a slight acceleration from 9% growth in Q3." For the full year, Experiences revenue grew 10% to $924 million, with GBV reaching more than $4.7 billion.

  • TheFork delivered Q4 revenue of $57 million, representing 18% growth, and full year revenue of $221 million, up 22%. Adjusted EBITDA for TheFork in Q4 was $1 million, with a 2% margin.

  • Hotels & Other revenue in Q4 declined 15% to $151 million; for the full year, it declined 8% to $750 million. Adjusted EBITDA in Hotels & Other was nearly $30 million in Q4.

  • For the full year, operating cash flow was $245 million and free cash flow was $163 million. The company ended December 31 with approximately $1 billion in cash and cash equivalents.

  • Tripadvisor repurchased 3.3 million shares in Q4 for a total of $50 million, reducing share count by 21% since the end of 2024.

Q&A

  • Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs, asked about incremental growth investments in Experiences and marketing strategy. Goldberg explained, "We see the Experiences market as very attractive. Obviously, it's growing faster than other travel categories."

  • Naved Khan, B. Riley Securities, questioned Experiences margin expansion and SEO reliance. Noonan replied, "We believe we can continue to drive efficiencies in our -- in marketing, driven by really product-driven conversion growth operating 2 teams, operating 2 platforms is one."

  • Nafeesa Gupta, BofA Securities, inquired about the AI-native MVP and AI partnerships. Goldberg responded, "Our goal is simple: utilize the substantial data and content we have to make more relevant personalized recommendations, better matched to travel intent and easier to book." Gupta also asked about economics from large AI platforms, to which Goldberg said, "We're seeing the conversions to be a bit stronger because it's relatively higher intent, we think, lower down the funnel."

  • Stephen Ju, UBS, asked about driving Viator usage in home markets. Goldberg said, "We're definitely continuing our work to attract customers who are interested in Experiences wherever they may be, whether that's on a long-haul trip, a domestic short-haul trip closer to home or even in Experiences that they want to have over a long weekend."

  • Lloyd Walmsley, Mizuho, focused on geographic expansion and AI partnerships. Goldberg explained, "Going after the geo expansion is very much about the supply that will appeal to international source markets that we haven't served in the past."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally inquisitive and focused on strategy, growth investments, and the role of AI, using a neutral to slightly positive tone.

  • Management tone during the prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing acceleration and transformation. During Q&A, Goldberg and Noonan maintained confidence, highlighting successful strategic execution and flexibility, with statements such as "we believe we can continue to drive efficiencies" and "we think that gives us the flexibility to invest further to drive global leadership."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remained focused on transformation and execution, with a slightly greater emphasis on Experiences and AI in the current quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter reinforced the transition towards Experiences as the primary business, with explicit plans to explore strategic alternatives for TheFork, which was not announced in the previous quarter.

  • Management's forward guidance language became more specific, projecting that marketplace businesses will make up approximately two-thirds of revenue in 2026, compared to the prior quarter's reference to a "steady" shift.

  • Analysts maintained focus on Experiences growth, AI strategy, and margin expansion, while management continued to express optimism about marketplace momentum, but provided increased detail on AI initiatives and supply growth.

  • Legacy Hotels & Other is now more explicitly positioned for simplification or potential exit, with continued cost alignment and profit optimization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted "structural demand headwinds" in legacy Hotels & Other, ongoing "traffic headwinds" due to SEO changes, and "anticipated declines in our legacy offering" in 2026.

  • Analyst questions reflected concerns about competitive intensity, marketing spend, margin trade-offs, and the evolving impact of AI on both user acquisition and monetization.

  • The company is watching for "higher seasonality trends" as Experiences and TheFork become a larger portion of revenue, and continues to evaluate "strategic options across the portfolio."

Final Takeaway

Tripadvisor management underscored that the company's transformation is well underway, with Experiences now at the heart of the business and set to drive the majority of revenue and profit in 2026. Strategic alternatives are being explored for TheFork to unlock further value. Management expressed confidence in the company's AI capabilities and ongoing cost discipline, while maintaining a clear focus on accelerating growth, expanding margins, and positioning Tripadvisor for long-term leadership in the global travel Experiences marketplace.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: LiveOne targets $85M–$95M revenue for fiscal 2027 as B2B pipeline and AI-driven efficiency accelerate growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LiveOne (LVO) Q3 2026

Management View

  • CEO Robert Ellin described the quarter as a “clear inflection point for our company,” highlighting over $20 million in Q3 revenue and more than $58 million for the first nine months. Ellin announced, “Operating expenses reduced by over 52% year-over-year. Our organization was streamlined with the help of AI from 350 people to 88 team members.” He emphasized structural changes including debt reduction, with more than $2.5 million paid off, and a shift toward a scalable, margin-expanding platform.
  • Ellin reported that the Audio Division generated $52.2 million in nine-month revenue and over $3.7 million in adjusted EBITDA, with Q3 alone delivering $18.6 million in revenue and $2.6 million of EBITDA. He introduced preliminary fiscal guidance for the first time: “$85 million to $95 million in revenues and $8 million to $10 million in adjusted EBITDA.”
  • B2B pipeline expansion was a key theme, with the pipeline “now the largest in company history, up over 30% in the last 120 days with over 100 active enterprise opportunities.” Ellin highlighted upcoming launches with three major Fortune 500 partnerships, two of which “have over 50 million monthly paying subscribers.”
  • Ellin noted intensified M&A interest: “We are also seeing a sharp acceleration in inbound M&A opportunities. As the market stabilizes and valuations normalize, strategic combinations are becoming increasingly attractive.”
  • Ellin discussed the forthcoming appointment of a new President “who has built and scaled and sold billion-dollar public companies.” This executive is expected to assume day-to-day operational roles, enabling Ellin to focus on B2B partnerships, M&A, and AI initiatives.
  • CFO Ryan Carhart stated, “Consolidated revenue for the 3-month period ended December 31, 2025, was $20.3 million. Our Audio Division posted revenue for Q3 of $18.6 million and adjusted EBITDA of $2.6 million.” Carhart added, “On a U.S. GAAP basis, LiveOne posted a consolidated net loss of $4.1 million or $0.37 per diluted share in Q3 fiscal 2026.”

Outlook

  • Ellin provided fiscal guidance for the first time, projecting “$85 million to $95 million in revenues and $8 million to $10 million in adjusted EBITDA” for fiscal 2027.
  • Management indicated that these projections are conservative and that the guidance “would only go up from there.” Carhart clarified, “the $85 million to $95 million is a very, very conservative look forward. So we would consider that to be a baseline case, a very baseline case, and it would only go up from there.”
  • Only a small portion of revenue from new B2B deals is included in guidance; Ellin specified, “nickels and dimes,” with full ramp expected in following quarters.

Financial Results

  • Carhart reported Q3 consolidated revenue of $20.3 million, with the Audio Division contributing $18.6 million and adjusted EBITDA of $2.6 million. Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was a positive $1.6 million.
  • LiveOne recorded a consolidated net loss of $4.1 million or $0.37 per diluted share. PodcastOne achieved record revenue of $15.9 million and adjusted EBITDA of $2.8 million, while Slacker reported $2.8 million in revenue and an adjusted EBITDA loss of $0.1 million.

Q&A

  • Barry Sine, Litchfield Hills Research, LLC, inquired about the customization of B2B deals and AI’s role in reducing staff. Ellin responded that “no 2 deals are alike,” emphasizing the company’s ability to white label and its nimbleness: “We’re the Walmart of the music space. Number two is we’re the most nimble... the ability that we have to be able to white label, right? None of those companies are going to give up their brands.” Sine also asked about Slacker’s ad-supported revenue. Ellin replied, “We partnered with DAX, the #1 programmatic advertising company in the world. We’ve just raised our ARPUs by over 30%.”
  • Brian Kinstlinger, Alliance Global Partners, questioned competitive dynamics and the status of B2B partnerships with large subscriber bases. Ellin asserted, “I don’t think there’s anybody else in the space that can do what we’re doing right now,” and confirmed the new Fortune 500 deals are “already signed.”
  • Kinstlinger also asked about revenue ramp for the three major B2B partnerships. Ellin explained the company is “trying to be super conservative” and that even a small conversion rate could drive significant revenue growth.
  • Sean McGowan, ROTH Capital Partners, sought clarity on cost sustainability. Carhart advised that current G&A and sales and marketing expenses “should absolutely model that forward, if not down,” with further reductions expected. McGowan also asked about the timing of revenue from new B2B deals. Carhart indicated a test phase would commence soon, with meaningful ramp expected the following year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the scalability of B2B deals, cost controls, advertising monetization, and the timing of new revenue streams, with a slightly positive but cautious tone. Questions probed details on partnership contracts, subscriber conversion, and operating leverage.
  • Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking during prepared remarks, with Ellin declaring, “Revenue is scaling, EBITDA is exploding. The earnings gap is closing. B2B partnerships are growing. AI initiatives are advancing.” In Q&A, management maintained confidence but exercised caution, repeatedly stressing conservative guidance and discipline in deal execution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from defensive to assertively optimistic, while analysts remained steady in their focus on execution and ramp timing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language evolved from expectations of future updates to explicit projections for fiscal 2027 ($85 million–$95 million revenue; $8 million–$10 million adjusted EBITDA).
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward B2B partnerships, AI-driven efficiency, and new leadership, while M&A interest intensified.
  • Analysts continued to probe the trajectory of B2B deals, subscriber conversion rates, and cost controls, with greater detail now available on partnership ramp-up and cost sustainability.
  • Key financial metrics reflected higher Q3 revenue and EBITDA, improved cost structure, and narrowing net loss compared to Q2.
  • Management’s confidence and forward momentum became more pronounced, especially regarding the scalability of B2B deals and AI integration.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ellin acknowledged the competitive risks posed by large streaming companies but emphasized LiveOne’s unique positioning through white labeling and B2B customization.
  • The company is monitoring the conversion of free to paid subscribers and expanding advertising revenue but recognizes the need to balance subscriber retention with ad monetization.
  • Carhart indicated ongoing efforts to further reduce G&A and OpEx, but acknowledged some variability in stock-based compensation depending on talent acquisition.
  • M&A and partnership execution risk remains, with management stressing disciplined evaluation to ensure shareholder value.

Final Takeaway

LiveOne’s leadership highlighted a transformative quarter marked by significant cost reductions, a narrowed net loss, and record results in its Audio and PodcastOne divisions. The company rolled out its first explicit fiscal guidance, targeting $85 million to $95 million in revenue and $8 million to $10 million in adjusted EBITDA for fiscal 2027, supported by a rapidly expanding B2B pipeline and AI-powered operational leverage. Management underscored imminent launches with major enterprise partners, a sharpened focus on scalable recurring revenue streams, and a proactive leadership transition to capitalize on accelerating strategic and M&A opportunities, all while maintaining a conservative approach to guidance and cost discipline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Churchill Capital Corp X shareholders approve business combination with Infleqtion
data.description:
• Churchill Capital Corp X (CCCX), a publicly traded special purpose acquisition company, on Thursday said its shareholders have approved the business combination with Infleqtion.
  • The support from Churchill X’s shareholders is expected to result in Infleqtion receiving over $550 million of gross proceeds, including nearly 100% of the cash held in Churchill X’s trust account before the redemption deadline and more than $125 million of incremental capital raised through a common stock PIPE at the transaction valuation from leading existing Infleqtion stockholders and new institutional investors.
  • Upon closing of the transaction, Infleqtion will become the first publicly listed neutral-atom quantum technology company, the company said.
  •  
  • Churchill X, whose shares of common stock, warrants, and units are currently listed on Nasdaq, will delist from Nasdaq.
  • Shares of common stock and warrants of the post-combination company, Infleqtion, will be listed on the New York Stock Exchange beginning on February 17, 2026, under the ticker symbols “INFQ” and “INFQ WS”, respectively. 
  • Prior to the closing of the transaction, each of the units sold by Churchill X in its initial public offering will be separated into one Class A ordinary share and one-quarter of one warrant of Churchill X and will no longer be listed on Nasdaq following the closing of the transaction.
  • The closing is expected to occur on February 13, 2026.
  • Infleqtion designs and builds quantum computers, precision sensors, and quantum software for governments, enterprises, and research institutions. 
  • CCCX +1.55% after hours to $13.42.
  • Source: Press Release

  • Revenue of $21.4M (+365.2% Y/Y).
